speaker: interviewer
text: 25th of May, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia. OK, camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about being moved from Ravensbruck into the German industry. Where were you actually taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I didn't know, but I was taken to a place called, um, Genshagen, Teltow, which was deceptive, because it was a-- to my-- the way I recollected. It was an ordinary sort of village. The camp itself, or the place they took me to, was fully underground. It was the, um, solution for German industry by then. We were very near Berlin. I think we were practically a suburb. I think since then, I found out that Teltow was, in fact, an outer suburb. And the bombings must have been hard and fast. The factory was fully, fully underground, equipped perfectly. It was aircraft engines. And we were-- a very small number, actually, were taken down. Every, um-- this type of industry had a quota system.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe the war was coming to an end. Who knows? We didn't know what the machinations were. Possibly, this was already a kind of insurance that they took people to different kind of work, other than the artificially created, back-breaking one that I had been to. I'm not sure, and I never found out.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, um, three stories deep. The dormitories were the lowest part. But they were actually not too bad. It was, um, I guess, like very low-class accommodation, stone-type of dormitories with a straw mattress, which we had to look after. And there were showers. There were really acceptable showers. We were very much encouraged and needn't have that-- didn't need to have the encouragement, because we wanted to be clean. We were pestered by then by lice and really, absolutely tormented by this. Again, we left there with freshly shaven heads. So it was bitterly cold in the factory. And, um, we worked on level two, I think. There were three levels. Level two is, I think, what we were working on. And my work consisted of being assigned to a foreman, together with about five others, who didn't all come with me. They were already there or came before me from other places, et cetera. Um, they didn't look too bad. Obviously, they were getting slightly more to eat. I remember the name of the man who I was assigned to was Mr. Peitch, with a German spelling, P-E-I-T-C-H. He was very, very polite. And I hoped that maybe my luck will change. He was, however, not really polite. He was just a machine. He knew how to behave. And he didn't quite know how to modify his behavior to us. He didn't know who-- what to do with us, how to handle us. He was so mystified by appearance. We had different prison clothes in that factory, no more attractive, but slightly less bizarre, perhaps. They were marked with a cross at the back. I'm quite sure that this must have been some kind of attempt at humane behavior. Because I developed a very bad ear infection. And because I had no hair, it was very visible that pus was pouring from my ear. And it stank to high heaven. And the people in charge complained that I was smelly. And one day-- I was quite sure that this was the end of me-- I was told that I'm going to be taken to a doctor. There was nobody like that on that particular set-up. And very early in the morning, I was taken by train with three others who needed medical treatment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a bizarre, bizarre occurrence, in my recollection. We were actually traveling on a civilian train through Berlin underground, in our prison clothes, no hair, no nothing, with a woman, a German army woman. And I could see the suburbs of Berlin rolling by that I had known from books I had read as a girl. And I thought, this is unreal. Where are we going?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we finished up in Oranienburg camp, where there was a Norwegian prison, or that was a men's camp. There was a Norwegian prison doctor, who actually treated my ear. I couldn't believe it. It was one of these complete inconsistencies over that period. And I was told that I had to come back for treatment. He did tell me I will never hear on that ear, that it's beyond redemption. Luckily, his prediction wasn't true. I have less hearing in one ear, but I can hear. And, um, he told me that he will keep the German woman occupied to give me something to eat. He was very, very nice. He was a prisoner, a Norwegian, who was there for harboring Jews. And, um, indeed, he got her a large brandy. I don't know. They were very, very well organized in that medical part of the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: He asked me a lot of questions. Did I know where we were? I had no idea. I couldn't tell him what the camp was called. It only sort of materialized later. He asked me, could I tell him what nationalities I was with? I couldn't tell him because we were not allowed to communicate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he gave me a plate of semolina, which as a child was mortal punishment to me. But I don't think anything ever tasted so delicious. And he promised to have it for me the next time, because he insisted I had to come back. And believe it or not, I had three visits by train with that woman, who probably went for her brandy. And the ear sort of got-- without any proper treatment-- they had no anti-- they had no antibiotics. All we had was sulfur powder or something. But it was cleaned out. And so I really felt that that period of being imprisoned was, on a scale of 1 to 10, closer to the positive, because it must have been some bizarre attempt of being able to show humanity. There was no other way. I didn't get beaten there, only once when I really, truly couldn't work. I was in agony with my ear, and I just couldn't. And a very polite man gave me a terrific whack on my face, on the face that hurt. So I do recall that. And it also was a period where I felt there's something very strange happening to-- to others. Because the man didn't turn up for work one day. And he was like clockwork. And he was an hour late, which was unbelievable. And he looked ashen. And when the break came, during which we got our daily ration of bread and something hot to drink, he ate his sandwich, which he called eine stuhle, which I have never heard before, and I never want to hear again. It's a slang word for sandwich.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he talked to me, which was very strange. He never did. And he said, and how is your ear? And I said, well, if he asks me a question, I can, too. And I said, and why were you late?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he gave me a dirty look. And he said, the American Allied armies bombed the house my wife and children were in. They are all dead. And I realized that I was sorry for him. I couldn't explain. In other words, um, try as one might, you didn't change. And I was really sorry for this man sitting there, not able to give in to his own, uh, personal, um, twist of fate. And he was still maintaining that, um, sense of duty and incredible dedication to whatever he was doing. I couldn't believe it. That was something that bothered me for years and years afterwards. It lasted for maybe an hour. And he was back to his tough and, in a way, very, very nasty self. In that place, we stayed till, I think, early April.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when one day we were becoming aware of terrific activity. And from our level of the accommodation, such as we had, we could look up to another level, which possibly was a-- a sort of, uh, office level. And-- sorry. No, that was the factory level, the middle one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we saw a huge activity of carrying records and papers and books and then an enormous bonfire. And we could not explain it any other way but that something is happening. Why are they burning things? And indeed, in a very short span of time, we were again marshalled and, at that stage, walked and walked and walked. And in between that walk, we were having train rides as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, I was no great hero-- ever. I still am not. And I was terrified of bombs. I think, other than really bad bashing, I was afraid of bombs beyond everything. And I-- I realized why. Because they were preceded by the air raid sirens, and I still to this day can't bear that sound. And I was just mortified of that sound. And we were in-- this time in open carriages, very unsupervised, come to think of it. But by then, probably we wouldn't have known how to resist or do anything anyway. And we were together with an enormous lot of white Russian-- uh, sorry-- Ukrainian women, non-Jewish, who were being dragged from labor camp to labor camp and were the most dehumanized I had ever seen and very, very vicious and very, very rough. And in fact, you had to be very lucky not to be beaten by them. And we traveled. And then there was a siren. And then there was enormous bombing. And the soldiers were running like mad, chooks all over the place. We could have so easily run away. I don't understand anymore why we didn't. Nobody did. Nobody even entered that opportunity in their heads at all. And we just waited till we were found, another leader of those that probably didn't disperse. And we walked and walked and walked and arrived after many, many days of torturous walk. I had dreadful, dreadful feet. And I was obviously getting very sick. I could feel that my body just was giving up. I really didn't want to go on anymore. And we finished up, strangely enough, outside the sentry box in Ravensbruck. I recognized the gate. I recognized the sentry box. And there was terror in my heart. I was really terrified we would have to start working in that quarry again. There, I met, at that point, when we were taken to Ravensbruck, um, someone who is still here in Australia, who was there, sitting and nursing her dying mother in her lap. And the mother eventually died. And she told me that things are not as bad there anymore as they were. I was taken to different barracks, to a different area of the camp that I had been to before. Um, and at that stage, I think I was wafting through various worlds. I couldn't sustain my fantasy world anymore of princes and princesses. And it was too difficult to live that life, of all the beautiful things that were going to happen later. And I got very ill with typhoid. And I remember just this uncontrollable stream of bowel action and the fear of being punished for doing just that. But it just all became impossible to control. I could no longer exercise anything over myself. And there we were, at that stage not working, but there was something far worse happening. Um, they just didn't know what to do with us. And the only way to handle us was to make us stand. And we stood and stood and stood for most of the day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] absolutely unable to maintain it. And then one day, there was a great flurry in the camp that the Red Cross was coming for a visit. And it became like another bizarre situation, where we were made to stand to attention, were told to make the best of our faces, were given normal clothes. And we were told if we carried off this farce of looking happy little people, we were going to get Red Cross parcels. It was terribly difficult. And indeed, a group of international Red Cross representatives were allowed at that stage, at that very late stage, into the camp. I don't know what they thought of us. But I shudder to think how they possibly could think we were normal human beings. Naturally, we got nothing at the end of it, nothing at all. We were just left to the normal routine. But we did get a feeling things were coming to an end. And then the day came, that fateful day-- I don't remember when it was-- when the Germans on the loud hailers told us that the camp is being vacated. They are leaving us there. Um, those who want to live are to come with them to the next camp. The ones that are not going to work with them will be, um, killed. And the killing was to be-- they showed us the detonators. They told us the whole camp was mined and we will be blasted into the air. And I think my decision was not just my decision. But a number of us could no longer face the ordeal. And we just decided-- chose to die. And that was it. And we lay down in the gutter and waited for death to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: They left us there. I remember, because somebody saw a clock in the office of the Germans. It was 2 o'clock in the afternoon. And that wait for that detonator was something I cannot describe. I just kept thinking, my mother's done it. My sister's done it. Why am I so, um, afraid of death? I guess I was, wafting in and out of consciousness, terrified. But nothing happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of the women who-- it was strange, because the camp looked completely deserted but for those who lay on the ground, just discharging feces all the time, uncontrollably. When I look at the pictures of [INAUDIBLE], so I realize that really, the action is exactly-- bodies are the same when they're completely debilitated. And out of nowhere, able-bodied people surfaced. And it transpired that a number of women who actually were office-bearers in Ravensbruck, working for the Germans, chose not to leave the camp, for whatever their reasons. I don't know. And I didn't care at that stage. And they started barking orders. And maybe that brought a kind of bearable reality into our situation again, because we followed those orders. They were women like us. But they were more able-bodied, mostly Czech, non-Jewish. And they said, well, we may as well die in dignity. And let's occupy the German barracks. I don't know what I occupied. But I was in a-- in a kind of a bunk bed. I couldn't make the highest bunk. I got to the middle. And the middle was window height. And I then recall nothing, only the fact that I woke up, and it was dark, and I was still alive, and there was no big bang. But I could hear the sounds of a motorbike. And I was sure I was hallucinating. And the motorbike was coming closer and closer and closer. And then I heard a voice cooing or making noise, noises as if waiting for responses. And none came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I understood Russian. I had learned it in camp. And I heard a Russian voice say, hm, they promised us girls. And there are no bloody girls here. Where are the girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a numbness, I suppose, in those that were able-bodied, because there was no response. We remained low without any sign of life, until he entered some barracks. I don't know which ones. And then there was this huge jubilation. It was a Russian soldier, the reconnaissance, the anti-climatic liberation of one body, one human being, after all that mass discipline and that mass barking and that mass everything. And he came and liberated. And after that, I don't remember much at all, until probably about a week later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you had to put a time frame on this, you'd say very early in May of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know the date. It was May 1.

speaker: interviewer
text: May 1 of 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They walked in on the night of 30 or 31st. I don't know how many days in April.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next day was May 1. That I was told. And a week later, I became aware of the fact that I was actually in a bed. I was in a hospital environment in the camp, which was the German, um, army's accommodation, the occupier's accommodation, which the Russians had, uh, fixed up for us who were sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was drifting in and out, in and out of consciousness, being aware of a predominance of cabbage smell, which was, um-- under the Germans, the predominance of food smell was, uh, turnip, which became something I don't ever want to smell again. This time, it was cabbage. And I thought, God, why? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was our life-saver. These people had absolutely the right attitude. And in spite of the fact that we all made the enormous blunder of hurling ourselves onto the Red Cross parcels that were there and devouring peanut butter and other absolute certain death food, they counteracted it with giving us huge amounts of cabbage to eat, which cleaned out, as best as it could, our systems, and were hands-on liberators. They were not, I'm sorry to say, like the Americans and the English who, I think, maybe couldn't handle so practically what they found as the Russians did, because they had gone through so much themselves. They were very good to us, very jovial. There were a number of Jewish people amongst them, keeping it very low key. I realized still that being Jewish is not something you-- you flaunted. And also, I recall that I couldn't get over the fact that they encouraged us to keep-- to get up. Although we were so ill, they encouraged us to get up and sort of barked to us to go to the latrines rather than, uh, do it here ourselves. And I often remember and often question at what moment it happened to me that I was able to go to these places and walk past ceiling-high piles of stiff human bodies, totally, totally anonymous, um, their eyes no longer seeing, but us seeing them, and not even take this as anything out of the ordinary. And I never looked for a familiar face. I never-- I never thought of them as being like I was. It bothers me hugely. But that's the way it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And very fortunately for me-- I must have had another bout of very bad typhoid, because I wasn't aware of the digging of graves and the getting rid of the bodies. Because when I came to again, I was sufficiently well to sit in the sun. And I remember sitting with my back to the barracks and then suddenly realizing I had adapted to that lot. How on Earth will I adapt to what was ahead?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the day when we were told that because of these Czech girls that were in the camp-- those who were of Czechoslovakian nationality are to be taken away, uh, first, back to their country, by means not yet known to us. We had very little friendship shown to us, um, by the Czech girls. Yes, they were very matter of fact. They did what was expected of them. But warmth, or what I was hoping for, was never forthcoming. And one day, they notified us that the trucks are on the way. And I think it must have been somewhere at the very end of, uh, May that, indeed, we set off on chauffeur-driven-- sorry, Czech-chauffeured trucks. Later on, I was told they were, in fact, camp inmates who had already been back and come to get us. I couldn't understand how they could have done it because the places were so totally, um, bombed, and the roads were nonexistent. But they did. They found a way of zig-zagging their way across. And I remember the trip back with bad memories, very bad memories, because the hostility of some of the Czechs was visible, painful. I came across the Norwegian doctor who helped my ear and his Czech assistant doctor. And the Czech assistant doctor who fed me and brought me, um, the food and encouragement was one of those who turned away when I came back free and made it very obvious to me that he had very little time for Jewish people. And I was extremely hurt. And when we finally arrived in Prague, there was-- probably my ignorance as to how people would handle returnees like me made it bitter, because we were again marshalled and again standing and again waiting for clothes and disinfecting. And I have a piece of paper from the quarantine station in Prague, which was probably hugely necessary, never mind my having had a bath and lived like a semi-human being in Ravensbruck. But I still had problems. And I still was very sick. And they had to do it. But I resented that much more than I resented anything else. And I finished up in a hospital in Prague, where I met a woman, who was somebody called Flora, who was a nurse there, and who, um-- who really forced me to face life again. And, um, she was pretty terrific.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make it back to Bratislava?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, It took a while, because in Prague, they discovered that I had TB. And they allowed me to go back on the proviso that, as I had already found out through the Red Cross, my father was alive in Palestine, my paternal uncles were alive and wanted me back, that for my-- my psyche, it would be better to first have some kind of a reunion before I was hospitalized again. This was terribly organized, I do recall, because I was really very, very weak. And I was sent back-- I can't remember whether it was by train, part of the way truck-- and then deposited at the Bratislava railway station, not knowing where they were. Nobody had notified. There was no communication possible. My uncle had no idea when I was coming, so he didn't wait for me. And I started-- sorry-- instinctively my trek to where he had lived when I last knew he was there, which was really very stupid because he had left, um, to be underground. And I had-- possibly I would have not found anybody there. But I had no choice. I set off on that track-- trek-- and hailed a Russian, then to be told afterwards that I must have been insane because they were so different to the Russians I had met. But I found only a kind of camaraderie. And they deposited me about a kilometer from where my uncle lived. That was as far as they went. And then I crawled on all fours. It was up hill all the way. I had absolutely no strength. And I remember coming to my uncle's house, to the house my uncle lived in, which had a tiny pocket-size front yard. And my little cousin Susan was in the garden. And I couldn't call out. I stood there for hours till she noticed me. She was a little girl of about two and a half, maybe three. And she pointed. And luckily, her father and mother came out and saw me. And that was my homecoming.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you actually stay in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I stayed in Czechoslovakia till, uh, 1949, '50.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you occupy yourself? What did you do in those intervening years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when my health got better again, the enormity of everything hit. And I was, I think, a real cripple. Because my fantasy life took over fully. I was, um, living in some unreal world. I wanted to go to school. And I found nothing that really suited me. And I compromised. I didn't want to go to school for business studies. I wish I had. But it was just such a horrible environment. I didn't know anyone. I didn't want to go there. And then I heard that another new school had started, which was an art school, as well as academic subjects. And I enrolled there, not knowing that that I would be the only girl. And I-- I stood it for two and a half years, and then it just became absolutely unbearable. I was tormented and teased. And anti-Semitism was obvious again. And I just couldn't bear it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I left that school and had private art tuition. I loved poetry. And I was reasonably good at it. I also learned, um, some more languages. And I also, um, had some kind of dressmaking course, for whatever it was worth.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father came back from Palestine in '46. And that was the final blow, because we had terrible trouble to find the relationship. My father was absolutely shattered. He-- he was totally unable to accept my mother and sister's death. And in his behavior, I think, he manifested it as a very stern father to me, to bring me in line. I think it was, in hindsight, very, very difficult for him, because he just didn't know how to handle me. But what I desperately, desperately resented was that nobody wanted to talk to me. Nobody wanted me to talk about anything, not one in the whole family. And so I was burying and burying everything and in this mixed up state couldn't possibly really concentrate on anything I was doing very well. I occupied myself. I worked. I tried to do anything and everything in my way. But it wasn't what I should have done, in hindsight. I couldn't come to terms with everyday life. I just couldn't. And, um, I formed a relationship in the search of love, I think, totally disastrously expecting one person to be able to, um, at age 23, be everything that I lost. And it was a doomed relationship because it was in the dream world sort of set-up that I had lived in, in that other world that wasn't really the one that I came back to. And when that relationship, after a very brief marriage, broke up, um, I realized that staying in Slovakia was my biggest problem because I was continuously reminded of that which had gone on. There was no grave. I didn't have the luxury of my mother, who for every little bit of grief, had a grave to go to, her father's and her mother's. And I just had nothing to hang anything onto. Um, I felt that being divorced was a stigma I couldn't live with. I felt totally unable to face life there. And contrary to my previous attitude to leaving Czechoslovakia for Australia, which my ex-husband then had wanted to do and did do-- he left and came to Australia-- I couldn't bear the thought of leaving my father once again. We had just managed to get close. And it was again my personal grief that brought me into his-- his arms. In a way, my-- my, uh, divorce and pain at losing a relationship opened up my father and me for each other. So I guess that was a kind of balance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Let's just stop there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of Tape 3 of the interview with Eva Grinston. It's the 25th of May, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia.
